## 6.5. LLM-powered active information gathering

Earlier we discussed how LLMs can help us with Passive Information Gathering. In this Learning Unit, we'll explore how LLMs can improve our approach with active enumeration, enabling us to uncover critical insights about a target's domain infrastructure with greater efficiency and precision.

DNS enumeration is a fundamental technique in reconnaissance, allowing us to query DNS servers and extract information such as subdomains, mail servers, and name servers. By integrating LLMs into this process, we can take DNS enumeration to the next level. LLMs can analyze query results, identify naming patterns, and correlate data across multiple sources, revealing misconfigurations or hidden assets. For instance, we can use LLMs to streamline the analysis of DNS zone transfers, reverse lookups, and WHOIS data, synthesizing findings into actionable intelligence faster than manual methods allow.

Info

Zone transfers replicate DNS data between servers. Misconfigured ones can leak internal data.

As mentioned in the previous LLM section on passive information gathering, as with any tool, LLMs have strengths and limitations. While they can simplify complex tasks and highlight connections we might miss, their outputs must be carefully validated for accuracy and relevance. It's also crucial to stay within ethical and legal boundaries when applying AI to active reconnaissance.

This Learning Unit covers the following Learning Objective:

* Active LLM-Aided Enumeration

## 6.5.1. Active LLM-aided enumeration

As we learned in the previous section about DNS enumeration, one of our first steps is to create a robust wordlist. In this section, we'll learn how to use LLMs to create effective wordlists for DNS enumeration. Building a strong wordlist is crucial to uncovering subdomains, services, or directories associated with a target domain. Traditionally, this process required us to manually sift through public information, but modern LLMs can streamline and enhance this step, helping us uncover patterns and generate better results.

Let's take **megacorpone.com** as an example again. We can start by asking ChatGPT to fetch any company's public data and filter that output to create a list of likely DNS subdomains that will make up our wordlist.

```
Using public data from MegacorpOne's website and any information that can be inferred about its organizational structure, products, or services, generate a comprehensive list of potential subdomain names.
	•	Incorporate common patterns used for subdomains, such as:
	•	Infrastructure-related terms (e.g., "api", "dev", "test", "staging").
	•	Service-specific terms (e.g., "mail", "auth", "cdn", "status").
	•	Departmental or functional terms (e.g., "hr", "sales", "support").
	•	Regional or country-specific terms (e.g., "us", "eu", "asia").
	•	Factor in industry norms and frequently used terms relevant to MegacorpOne's sector.

Finally, compile the generated terms into a structured wordlist of 1000  words, optimized for subdomain brute-forcing against megacorpone.com

Ensure the output is in a clean, lowercase format with no duplicates, no bulletpoints and ready to be copied and pasted.
Make sure the list contains 1000 unique entries.
```

> Listing 67 - LLM's prompt to generate a DNS subdomain wordlist

Here, we specified in the LLM-prompt the output format and the wordlist size as well.

The answer we receive from the LLM is quite verbose, but we can focus on the most relevant bit of it.

```
I have generated a structured 1000-word subdomain wordlist optimized for brute-forcing against megacorpone.com. You can download the file for use directly:

Download Subdomain Wordlist
```

> Listing 68 - LLM's response with the DNS subdomain wordlist

In this scenario, the LLM attached the wordlist to the response so we can download it via the LLM-provided link to our local Kali machine and save it as *wordlist.txt*. We could simply copy and paste the list if it were included directly in the response.

We should again keep in mind that the LLM's answer may vary, such as providing a shorter list. Persistence, even with LLMs, pays off, so we should repeat our request if the output doesn't meet our needs.

In order to speed up our DNS enumeration, we can install [*gobuster*](https://www.kali.org/tools/gobuster/) on our Kali machine, which is an open-source, command-line tool designed for fast and efficient brute-forcing and enumeration of different kinds of resources.

Before running our DNS brute-force enumeration, we need to install it on Kali, as follows:

```
sudo apt update
sudo apt install gobuster
```

> Listing 69 - Installing gobuster on Kali

With gobuster installation out of the way, we will issue *gobuster dns -d megacorpone.com -w wordlist -t 10* to perform a DNS brute-forcing operation on the domain megacorpone.com.

The *dns* option will specify that we are utilizing Gobuster's DNS module. By including *-d megacorpone.com*, we will direct the tool to target this specific domain for our enumeration.

The *-w wordlist.txt* argument will allow us to supply the custom LLM-generated wordlist. Finally, the *-t 10* parameter will define the level of concurrency, setting Gobuster to use 10 threads simultaneously to improve efficiency.

```
kali@kali:~$ gobuster dns -d megacorpone.com -w wordlist.txt -t 10
===============================================================
Gobuster v3.6
by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@firefart)
===============================================================
[+] Domain:     megacorpone.com
[+] Threads:    10
[+] Timeout:    1s
[+] Wordlist:   wordlist
===============================================================
Starting gobuster in DNS enumeration mode
===============================================================
[INFO] [-] Unable to validate base domain: megacorpone.com (lookup megacorpone.com on 8.8.8.8:53: no such host)
Found: admin.megacorpone.com

Found: router.megacorpone.com

Found: mail.megacorpone.com

Found: support.megacorpone.com

Found: test.megacorpone.com
...
```

> Listing 70 - Running gobuster DNS subdomain enumeration with our LLM-generated wordlist

Info

Gobuster > 3.6 uses the --do flag for domains instead. We can always doublecheck the help page when in doubt about a specific tool usage.

As we expected, Gobuster discovered five valid subdomains by using a wordlist generated with the assistance of an LLM.

As offensive security practitioners, we've observed how LLMs can transform the way we approach both active and passive enumeration. By integrating LLMs into our workflow, we can improve processes, enhance efficiency, and uncover more valuable insights during reconnaissance.

In active enumeration, such as our DNS brute-forcing example, we used an LLM to generate a tailored wordlist. This wordlist was customized based on the inferred structure and naming patterns of the target organization, significantly increasing the likelihood of discovering valid subdomains. Traditionally, we would have spent significant time manually compiling such lists or relying on generic datasets, but LLMs allowed us to automate this step and create a highly focused and customized wordlist.

#### Labs

Text Answer

1. How does the use of LLMs improve the DNS enumeration process?

```
A) By automatically validating discovered subdomains.
B) By generating highly customized wordlists based on target-specific patterns.
C) By running DNS brute-forcing tools faster than traditional methods.
D) By identifying and exploiting vulnerabilities in the domain.
```

Your answer

## 6.6. Wrapping up

In this Learning Module, we explored the foundational aspects of the iterative processes of both passive and active information gathering. We first covered a variety of techniques and tools to locate information about companies and their employees. This information can often prove to be invaluable in later stages of the engagement. We then focused on how to actively scan and enumerate services that are commonly exposed. We learned how to perform these enumeration steps from both Kali Linux and a Windows client.

There is never one "best" tool for any given situation, especially since many tools in Kali Linux overlap in function. It's always best to familiarize ourselves with as many tools as possible, learn their nuances, and whenever possible, measure the results to understand what's happening behind the scenes. In some cases, the "best" tool is the one held by the pentester who is most familiar with it.

# 7. Vulnerability Scanning

In this Learning Module, we will cover the following Learning Units:

* Vulnerability Scanning Theory
* Vulnerability Scanning with Nessus
* Vulnerability Scanning with Nmap

[*Vulnerability scanning*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vulnerability_scanner) is the automated process of probing a system, network, or application for known weaknesses and misconfigurations.

Vulnerability scanners range from simple scripts that identify a single vulnerability to complex commercial solutions that assess a broad range of attack vectors. An attack vector is a path or method an attacker can use to gain unauthorized access, while the attack surface is the collection of all exposed entry points through which an attacker could enter a system or extract data. We focus on network scanners in this module because they form the foundation of most penetration-testing engagements and complement the web-specific techniques covered elsewhere in PEN-200.

Automated vulnerability scanners are invaluable for penetration testers because they quickly establish a baseline of potential weaknesses before deeper manual testing begins. The two most common scanner types are **web application** scanners, which focus on HTTP-based attack surfaces, and **network** scanners, which probe hosts, ports, and services across a network.

In this module, we will utilize automated network vulnerability scanning. We'll begin with the theory behind vulnerability scanning and then use [*Nessus*](https://www.tenable.com/products/nessus) and [*Nmap*](https://nmap.org) to perform different kinds of vulnerability scans.

## 7.1. Vulnerability scanning theory

This learning unit introduces the foundational concepts behind vulnerability scanning: the workflow, scan types, and planning considerations that apply regardless of the tool used.

Learning Objectives:

* Understand the vulnerability scanning process
* Learn about the different types of vulnerability scans
* Understand the considerations of a vulnerability scan

In this Learning Unit, we'll discuss vulnerability scanning theory. First, we'll outline the basic workflow of a vulnerability scanner and understand how it finds vulnerabilities. Then, we will review the different types and considerations of a vulnerability scan.

## 7.1.1. How vulnerability scanners work

Each vulnerability scanner follows its own customized workflow, but the core process is the same:

1. Host discovery
2. Port scanning
3. Operating system, service, and version detection
4. Matching the results to a vulnerability database

These steps allow the scanner to identify exposed services and determine whether known vulnerabilities affect them.

Together, these four steps give the scanner enough context to match discovered services against known vulnerabilities.

[*Host discovery*](https://capec.mitre.org/data/definitions/292.html) determines whether the target system is reachable and responding on the network. The scanner then probes all open ports to detect remotely accessible services and their versions, and also identifies the operating system.

Using the collected information, the scanner queries a vulnerability database to match the detected software and versions against known vulnerabilities.

Examples of vulnerability databases are the [*National Vulnerability Database*](https://nvd.nist.gov) (NVD) and the [*Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures*](https://cve.mitre.org/cve/search_cve_list.html) (CVE) program. CVE lists and names specific vulnerabilities. NVD builds on that list and adds severity scores and deeper analysis for each vulnerability.

Most commercial vulnerability scanners also have the functionality to verify found vulnerabilities by attempting to partially or fully exploit them. This can significantly reduce missed vulnerabilities but can impact the stability of the service or system.

A CVE identifier tells us *what* the vulnerability is, but not *how serious* it is. That is the role of the Common Vulnerability Scoring System.

The [*Common Vulnerability Scoring System*](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss) (CVSS) is a framework for addressing the characteristics and severity of vulnerabilities. Each CVE has a CVSS score assigned. The two major versions are [CVSS v3](https://www.first.org/cvss/user-guide) and [CVSS v4](https://www.first.org/cvss/v4.0/user-guide).

Both versions use a range from 0 to 10 to rate vulnerabilities with different severity labels. The following figure from the [*National Institute of Standards and Technology*](https://www.nist.gov/) (NIST) lists the range of the base score and associated severity for CVSS v2.0, CVSS v3.0 and CVSS v4.0.

![Figure 1: CVSS Ratings](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/ba62f823573b01280d732a28d0eb64af-vulnscan_cvssv1a.png)

Figure 1: CVSS Ratings

Source: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), NVD.

To obtain a CVSS score, we can review the CVE entry in a vulnerability database or use an online CVSS calculator. NIST provides calculators for both [*CVSS v3 calculator*](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss/v3-calculator) and [*CVSS v4 calculator*](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss/v4-calculator).

CVSS v4.0 improves scoring accuracy by clarifying attack conditions, modeling downstream impact, and reducing ambiguity present in CVSS v3.1.

Vulnerability scan results can be incomplete or contain inaccurately reported findings. Two key error types to understand are false positives and false negatives.

A [*false positive*](https://www.cgisecurity.com/questions/falsepositive.shtml) occurs when the scanner reports a vulnerability that does not actually affect the target. This can result from incorrect service or version detection, or from a configuration that makes the target unexploitable. False positives also occur when security fixes are [*backported*](https://access.redhat.com/security/updates/backporting) — that is, applied to an older software version — because the scanner sees the old version string and flags it as vulnerable.

A [*false negative*](https://www.cgisecurity.com/questions/falsenegative.shtml) is the opposite: a real vulnerability that the scanner fails to detect.

In a penetration test, we often need to find the right balance between manual and automated vulnerability scanning.

A manual vulnerability scan is very resource-intensive and time-consuming. When analyzing large amounts of data, we often reach our cognitive limit quickly and overlook vital details. On the other hand, manual vulnerability scanning allows for the discovery of complex and logical vulnerabilities that are rather difficult to discover using any type of automated scanner.

Automated vulnerability scans are valuable during engagements for several reasons:

* establishing a baseline
* scaling assessments across many hosts
* working within time constraints
* identifying low-hanging fruit

Take time to understand how each automated tool works before using it in a security assessment. That effort helps configure the tool correctly and interpret the results in a meaningful way. It also reveals where the tool falls short and where manual expertise needs to fill the gaps.

#### Labs

1. Is this result a false positive or a false negative? A vulnerability scanner identifies a vulnerability for a Linux web server. The target runs on Windows and the vulnerability is only exploitable on Linux.

Answer

2. Is this a false positive or a false negative? A vulnerability scanner detects the wrong version of an FTP service. The detected version has no vulnerabilities, but the running FTP service is vulnerable.

Answer

## 7.1.2. Types of vulnerability scans

Beyond understanding how scanners work, we also need to know which type of scan to run for a given engagement.

This section looks at vulnerability scans from two angles: where the scan runs (internal or external) and what level of access it uses (unauthenticated or authenticated).

If a client tasks us with an *`external`* vulnerability scan, they mean to analyze one or more systems that are accessible from the internet. Targets in an external vulnerability scan are often web applications, systems in the [*demilitarized zone*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DMZ_\(computing\)) (DMZ), and public-facing services.

The client wants a clear picture of the security status of all systems that an external attacker can reach. In most cases, the client provides a list of IP addresses for the scan. Sometimes, the client asks the team to identify all externally accessible systems and services on its own.

A company should always know which systems are publicly reachable. In practice, that awareness often falls short. As a result, the team frequently discovers sensitive external systems and services that the company did not realize were exposed.

If a client tasks us with an **internal** vulnerability scan, we either get [*VPN*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virtual_private_network) access or perform the scan on-site. The intention is to get an overview of the security status of the internal network. It is important to analyze which vectors an attacker can use after breaching the perimeter.

The next two scan types we will examine are authenticated and unauthenticated vulnerability scans.

An **unauthenticated** vulnerability scan is done on a system without credentials. These are geared towards finding vulnerabilities in remotely accessible services on a target. Therefore, they map the system with all open ports and provide us with an attack surface by matching the information to vulnerability databases as mentioned before.

Since we are on the outside looking in, we get no information about local security flaws, such as missing patches, outdated software, or configuration vulnerabilities on the system itself.

For example, an unauthenticated vulnerability scan on a Windows target could not determine whether the system is patched against the [*HiveNightmare*](https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2021-36934) vulnerability. This is where authenticated scans come into play. Unauthenticated scans assume that all vulnerabilities are visible from the network. That assumption breaks down for local vulnerabilities, which require the scanner to log in and inspect the system directly.

Most scanners can be configured to run **authenticated** scans. Here the scanner logs in to the target with a set of valid credentials.

In most instances, authenticated scans use a privileged user account to have the best visibility into the target system. The goal of authenticated vulnerability scans is to check for vulnerable packages, missing patches, or configuration vulnerabilities.

We will perform both authenticated and unauthenticated scans in the next Learning Unit, but first, let's discuss how to obtain accurate and conclusive results.

#### Labs

1. Do you need to perform an authenticated or an unauthenticated vulnerability scan in the following scenario? You want to determine if all current patches on a Linux system are installed.

Answer

2. Do you need to perform an authenticated or an unauthenticated vulnerability scan in the following scenario? You want to analyze the perimeter of a server on the internet from the perspective of a malicious actor.

Answer

## 7.1.3. Things to consider in a vulnerability scan

This section explains the key points to consider when planning and running a vulnerability scan. On larger engagements, the team configures the vulnerability scanner carefully, so the results stay meaningful and relevant.

First is the *scanning duration*. Depending on the scanning type and number of targets, the duration of an automated scan can vary greatly. Because external scans over the internet can be time-consuming due to the number of hops and intermediate systems on the network route, we must plan accordingly if we have a large list of IP addresses.

Next is *target visibility*. While it is easy to input an IP address and start the vulnerability scan, we often must properly consider our targets. It's important to determine if our targets are accessible without the need for any VPNs or permissions in a firewall.

In most cases, a client-provided list of IP addresses for an external scan is not a concern. However, when we are responsible for defining the attack surface of a client’s publicly accessible infrastructure, we must account for firewalls and other access controls that may limit visibility into systems and services.

For example, an international client has several systems in multiple countries. They restrict access from all IP addresses outside of the country where each system is located. From our location, we are only able to access the systems located in our country while all others are inaccessible to us.

Let's also consider target visibility in an internal engagement. We need to think about our positioning in the network to get meaningful results, especially when we want to scan systems from other subnets. Keep in mind that firewalls, [*intrusion prevention systems*](https://www.vmware.com/topics/glossary/content/intrusion-prevention-system.html) (IPS), and intermediate network devices (such as routers), can filter or alter our traffic. One example of this is when a vulnerability scanner sends ICMP packets in the Host Discovery step and the intermediate device does not forward them. Hence, the scanner marks the target as offline.

In addition, our scan can be affected by [*rate limiting*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rate_limiting), which is used to limit the amount of traffic on a network. When our scan exceeds thresholds like throughput, packet count, or connection count, the source system of our vulnerability scan can be drastically restricted in the context of networking capabilities. When the host discovery and service detection probes are rate-limited and therefore slowed down, the vulnerability scanner may miss live hosts or services. Most vulnerability scanners can address this by specifying delays, timeouts, and limiting parallel connections.

Finally, let's review the network and system impact of vulnerability scans. A vulnerability scanner generates significant network traffic, especially when scanning multiple targets in parallel. This can saturate the network and make it unusable for other users. To mitigate this, we can reduce the number of parallel scans or lower the scanning speed.

An equally important concern is the potential impact on the stability of scanned systems. Vulnerability scans can trigger crashes or service disruptions — even on healthy systems — so we must exercise appropriate caution on production targets.

#### Labs

1. Is the following statement true or false? A vulnerability scan can never impact the stability of a target system or service.

Answer

2. Is the following statement true or false? Rate limiting can be the reason that a vulnerability scanner can flag a live target system as offline.

Answer

## 7.2. Vulnerability scanning with Nessus

With the theory in place, we'll put it into practice using Nessus, one of the most widely used commercial vulnerability scanners.

This Learning Unit covers the following Learning Objectives:

* Install Nessus
* Understand the different Nessus components
* Configure and perform a vulnerability scan
* Understand and work with the results of a vulnerability scan with Nessus
* Perform an authenticated vulnerability scan
* Gain a basic understanding of Nessus plugins

In this learning unit, we’ll focus on Nessus, one of the most widely used vulnerability scanners, with coverage for over 67,000 [CVEs](https://cve.mitre.org) across more than [168,000 plugins](https://www.tenable.com/plugins).

[Nessus](https://www.tenable.com/products/nessus) is available in several editions: *Nessus Essentials*, *Nessus Essentials Plus*, and *Nessus Professional*. In this module, we will use the free *Nessus Essentials* version, which includes some limitations such as scanning up to five IP addresses and reduced feature availability.

Warning

Because we are limited to five IP scans, you should complete this module using a single VPN connection for the best results. This module uses five different host IPs, which exactly matches the number of IP scans available.

Restarting the VPN connection increases the likelihood that the third octet of the host machine IP will change, which could cause you to exceed the five-scan limit.

Nessus Essentials was recently split into two tiers: *Essentials* and *Essentials Plus*. Some capabilities, including scanning up to 20 IP addresses and expanded reporting features, are now available only in the Plus version.

Despite these limitations, the standard Essentials version still provides a practical introduction to Nessus and its workflow. The concepts covered in this section also apply to the full commercial version and to most enterprise vulnerability scanners.

## 7.2.1. Installing Nessus

For this Learning Unit, we'll install Nessus on our Kali Linux VM used to connect to the PEN-200 lab environment. Downloading and activating Nessus requires an internet connection and a business email address. The minimum hardware requirements [*Tenable* recommends](https://docs.tenable.com/generalrequirements/Content/NessusScannerHardwareRequirements.htm) are 4 CPU cores and 8GB of RAM. However, we don't need to meet those requirements for our exercises. 2 CPU cores and 4GB of RAM are sufficient for our needs.

Nessus is not available in the Kali repositories and needs to be installed manually. We can download the current version of Nessus as a 64-bit [*.deb*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deb_\(file_format\)) file for Kali from the [Tenable website](https://www.tenable.com/downloads/nessus). There, we also get the [*SHA256*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SHA-2) and [*MD5*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MD5) checksums for the installer.

Learners using a Kali VM with an ARM-based chip should select the **Linux** - **Ubuntu** - **aarch64** platform.

Learners using an x64 based Kali VM should select the **Linux** - **Debian**

* **amd64** platform.

![Figure 2: Download Nessus for Kali](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/88e2d7b57869350752892c26532b5e19-vulnscan_nessusdownloadr2.png)

Figure 2: Download Nessus for Kali

When done, we can check the SHA256 checksum to validate it. To do this, we click the **Checksum** button and copy the SHA256 checksum to the clipboard via the copy icon.

We then `echo` the copied checksum together with the filename of the installer into a file with the name `sha256sum_nessus`. Since the button next to the SHA256 checksum only copies the checksum itself, we need to enter the file name manually. The resulting `sha256sum_nessus` file needs to be in the same directory as the Nessus installer. We will then use [`sha256sum`](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/sha256sum.1.html) with the `-c` parameter to verify the checksum. A result of OK confirms the download is intact.

```
kali@kali:~$ cd ~/Downloads

kali@kali:~/Downloads$ echo "4987776fef98bb2a72515abc0529e90572778b1d7aeeb1939179ff1f4de1440d Nessus-10.5.0-debian10_amd64.deb" > sha256sum_nessus

kali@kali:~/Downloads$ sha256sum -c sha256sum_nessus
Nessus-10.5.0-debian10_amd64.deb: OK
```

> Listing 1 - Verifying the checksum

The output shows that the checksums match. If there is an updated version of Nessus, the checksum will differ and must be updated accordingly.

To install the package, we'll use [`apt`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/APT_\(software\)) with the `install` option. The output confirms a successful installation and displays the command to start the service. Note that the version shown may not be the most current.

```
kali@kali:~/Downloads$ sudo apt install ./Nessus-10.5.0-debian10_amd64.deb
...
Preparing to unpack .../Nessus-10.5.0-debian10_amd64.deb ...
Unpacking nessus (10.5.0) ...
Setting up nessus (10.5.0) ...
...
Unpacking Nessus Scanner Core Components...
 - You can start Nessus Scanner by typing /bin/systemctl start nessusd.service
 - Then go to https://kali:8834/ to configure your scanner
```

> Listing 2 - x64 Nessus installation

After the installation is complete, we can start the `nessusd` service via [`systemctl`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Systemd). This command produces no output on success — if the service fails to start, `systemctl status nessusd.service` will show the reason.

```
kali@kali:~/Downloads$ sudo systemctl start nessusd.service
```

> Listing 3 - Starting Nessus

Once Nessus is running, we can launch a browser and navigate to `https://127.0.0.1:8834`. We will be presented with a warning indicating an unknown certificate issuer, which is expected due to a self-signed certificate. To accept and trust the self-signed certificate, we can click on **Advanced...** and then **Accept the Risk and Continue**.

![Figure 3: Nessus Presenting a Certificate Warning](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/44c0a5eb2f5a1c9b1038ad4b790a0400-vulnscan_nessuscertwarningr.png)

Figure 3: Nessus Presenting a Certificate Warning

We may need to wait briefly for the initialization process to complete before the page loads.

After the page loads, we are prompted to configure pre-installation settings. Let's click on **Continue** to start the installation with the default settings.

![Figure 4: Configuring Pre-Installation Settings](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/e0d95ed9288d88420e9298f6a6a99064-vulnscan_nessusselectpreinstall2.png)

Figure 4: Configuring Pre-Installation Settings

Now, we can select a Nessus product. For this Learning Unit, we'll choose **Register for Nessus Essentials** and click **Continue**.

![Figure 4: Selecting Nessus Essentials](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/e8446c2900046d5ffbe6c4c6e0a313ed-vulnscan_nessusselectversionr5a.png)

Figure 4: Selecting Nessus Essentials

Next, we are prompted to request an activation code for Nessus Essentials. We'll provide the required information and click **Register**.

![Figure 5: Requesting an Activation Code](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/1b426f5fc6d94f113d657e6ba93fac4f-vulnscan_nessusactivationr5.png)

Figure 5: Requesting an Activation Code

Once we have registered, the activation code is shown in the next window.

![Figure 6: Activating Nessus](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/9012ed6308250bdaaa4dcd83f8afff75-vulnscan_nessusregisterr5.png)

Figure 6: Activating Nessus

Next, we'll create a local Nessus user account. We'll choose the username *admin* with a strong password to protect our vulnerability scan results. We'll use these credentials to log in to the Nessus application.

![Figure 7: Creating a Local Nessus Account](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/7f7af8ca63c58b815a2d02a3f4e187eb-vulnscan_nessususercreationr5.png)

Figure 7: Creating a Local Nessus Account

Finally, Nessus downloads and compiles all plugins. This process can take up to 30 minutes, depending on internet connection speed.

![Figure 8: Downloading Nessus Plugins](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/91b409866659812f883017412f5752e2-vulnscan_nessuscompilationr3.png)

Figure 8: Downloading Nessus Plugins

After the plugins are downloaded and installed, we have a working instance of Nessus Essentials.

We will be greeted by a "Welcome to Nessus Essentials" popup as shown below.

![Figure 9: Welcome to Nessus Essentials](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/1773f8dff4c8a78b4a1299b07458b36d-vulnscan_welcome.png)

Figure 9: Welcome to Nessus Essentials

The most important thing to not here is "Hosts that are discovered through a discovery scan do not count towards the 5 host limit on your license."

We now have Nessus installed.

Let's move on and examine the components it has. Click close to move around the UI.

#### Labs

1. Follow the steps above to install Nessus Essentials. What is the command to start the nessusd service after a successful installation from an unprivileged account?

Answer

## 7.2.2. Nessus components

Before our first scan, let's take some time to get familiar with the core components. When we log in for the first time, we are presented with a welcome window where we can enter scan targets directly — we'll do that shortly.

First, let's investigate the tabs in the Nessus dashboard. At the top of the dashboard, we have two tabs: **Scans** and **Settings**.

![Figure 10: Nessus dashboard — Scans and Settings tabs](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/648c454f38d9c2db68a8e3189068ccb2-vulnscan_nessusdashboardsettingsra.png)

Figure 10: Nessus dashboard — Scans and Settings tabs

The **Settings** tab allows us to configure the application. When we click on that, we can enter different information. For example, we can enter information for an [*SMTP server*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simple_Mail_Transfer_Protocol) to get scan results via email. The **Advanced** menu allows us to configure global settings ranging from user interface, scan, and log behavior to security and performance-related options.

![Figure 11: Nessus Settings — About menu and license information](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/4ce14b6303fc7767b9f33f8267ce37ad-vulnscan_nessusdashboardsettingsrb.png)

Figure 11: Nessus Settings — About menu and license information

As shown in Figure 11, the **About** menu lists basic information for Nessus, our license, and how many *Licensed Hosts* we have left. For further information on how we can customize and configure Nessus, we can consult the [Nessus documentation](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/Settings.htm).

Next, let's examine policies and templates, by clicking on the **Scan** tab, and then **Policies**, located under the **Resources** header. A policy is a set of predefined configuration options in the context of a Nessus scan. When we save a policy, we can use it as a template for a new scan.

Let's now click on **Scan Templates**. Nessus already provides a broad variety of [scanning templates](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/ScanAndPolicyTemplates.htm) for us to use. These templates are grouped into the three categories **Discovery**, **Vulnerabilities**, and **Compliance**.

![Figure 12: Nessus Policy Templates](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/e7c29ac6d27cfb4198e84f304e07d6cf-vulnscan_nessustemplatedashboard2r.png)

Figure 12: Nessus Policy Templates

The **Compliance** category and the **Mobile Device Scan** template are only available in the Nessus Professional version of Nessus. There are only two templates in the **Discovery** category, **Host Discovery** and **Ping-Only Discover**. We will focus on the **Host Discovery** scan, which can be used to create a list of live hosts and their open ports.

The **Vulnerabilities** category consists of templates for critical vulnerabilities or vulnerability groups — such as [*PrintNightmare*](https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2021-34527) or [*Zerologon*](https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2020-1472) — as well as templates for common scanning areas such as **Web Application Tests** or **Malware Scans**.

Nessus also provides three general vulnerability scanning templates:

1. The *Basic Network Scan* performs a full scan with most settings predefined. It will detect a broad variety of vulnerabilities and is therefore the recommended scanning template by Nessus. We also have the option to customize these settings and recommendations.

2. The *Advanced Scan* is a template without any predefined settings. We can use this when we want to fully customize our vulnerability scan or if we have specific needs.

3. The last general scanning template, *Advanced Dynamic Scan*, also comes without any predefined settings or recommendations.

The key difference between the *Advanced Scan* and *Advanced Dynamic Scan* templates is that the Advanced Dynamic Scan does not require manually selecting plugins. Instead, it allows us to configure a [*dynamic plugin filter*](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/DynamicPlugins.htm).

Nessus Plugins are programs written in the [*Nessus Attack Scripting Language*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nessus_Attack_Scripting_Language) (NASL) that contain the information and the algorithm to detect vulnerabilities. Each plugin is assigned to a [*plugin family*](https://www.tenable.com/plugins/families/about), which covers different use cases.

We will work with the Advanced Dynamic Scan template and plugins in the last section of this Learning Unit.

#### Labs

1. What is the third group of template categories: DISCOVERY, COMPLIANCE and \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?

Answer

2. Go to the **Settings** tab in Nessus then click on the **Advanced** settings. Find how many concurrent web users are allowed with the default settings.

Answer

## 7.2.3. Performing a vulnerability scan

Now that we’re familiar with the Nessus interface and template options, we can configure and launch our first vulnerability scan by clicking the **New Scan** button on the dashboard in the **Scans** tab.

![Figure 13: Creating a Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/0c4a555ea4542d0c738969e4c9bd56b7-vulnscan_nessusnewscanr.png)

Figure 13: Creating a Scan

Nessus provides a list of the different templates. For this section, we will use the **Basic Network Scan**, which we can launch by clicking on it.

![Figure 14: Selecting a Basic Network Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/9fac4fadd9a5c36adade63375edef99f-vulnscan_nessustemplateselectscanr.png)

Figure 14: Selecting a Basic Network Scan

This will present the scan [configuration settings](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/TemplateSettings.htm) screen containing the **BASIC**, **DISCOVERY**, **ASSESSMENT**, **REPORT**, and **ADVANCED** settings.

![Figure 15: Different Settings in Scan Configuration](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/e2ea0bb357323c2bb4a451ecf5ae0305-vulnscan_basicsettingsmenu2.png)

Figure 15: Different Settings in Scan Configuration

The default screen is the **General** settings page with the two required arguments: a name for our scan and a list of targets. Nessus supports [multiple target specifications](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/ScanTargets.htm), including a single IP address, an IP range, a comma-delimited list, and a *Fully-Qualified Domain Name* (FQDN).

For this example, we will scan the following machines: POULTRY, JENKINS, WK01, and SAMBA. We will enter "Basic Vulnerability Scan" into the **Name** field and the IP addresses of the machines into the **Targets** field.

![Figure 16: Configuring Scan Name and Target List](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/2642795aebee2c1b362e5260c0c31765-vulnscan_unauthiplisttarget6r.png)

Figure 16: Configuring Scan Name and Target List

Since we chose the Basic Network Scan template, Nessus has already configured most of the settings for us. However, the default configuration might not be exactly what we need.

Depending on the scanning type, the environment, time constraints, and the targets, we may need to adapt the settings to fit our needs.

In the default settings of this template, Nessus scans a list of common ports. For this demonstration, we only want to scan ports 80 and 443. To do this, let's start by clicking on the **Discovery** settings.

![Figure 17: Selecting Custom Discovery Settings](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/46ea8598c2f58c65007fa30ea0fd0ed1-vulnscan_nessusmenudiscoverycustomr.png)

Figure 17: Selecting Custom Discovery Settings

The dropdown menu shown in Figure 17 provides us with several predefined options. To scan specific ports, we'll need to select **Custom**.

After we click on **Custom**, additional configuration menus appear under the **DISCOVERY** menu. We can now customize the Basic Network Scan template in the same way as the Advanced Scan template in the context of the **DISCOVERY** menu. Within the **Port Scanning** section, we will set the **Port scan range** to "80,443". Additionally, we'll enable the **Consider unscanned ports as closed** option so that Nessus treats other ports as closed since we are only interested in ports 80 and 443.

![Figure 18: Specifying Ports 80 and 443](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/f95dd4cd35e01e8e833ca4dadbdb5831-vulnscan_unauthport4432r.png)

Figure 18: Specifying Ports 80 and 443

In this demonstration, we've customized the Basic Network Scan template to only scan two specific TCP ports. But even in the default settings of this template, Nessus does not scan UDP ports. If we want to activate UDP port scanning, we need to manually configure it. We may miss crucial information on UDP services when it's disabled during assessments, but we need to understand that activating UDP port scanning will vastly increase the scan duration. Due to the nature of UDP, it is not often possible to tell the difference between an open and a filtered port.

To save time and scan the targets more quietly, we will turn off **Host Discovery** because we know the hosts are available. We do this by navigating to **Discovery** > **Host Discovery** where we toggle **Ping the remote host** to **OFF**.

![Figure 19: Disable Host Ping in Discovery Settings](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/56c98995153da06465db4563663c1aea-vulnscan_nessushostdiscr2.png)

Figure 19: Disable Host Ping in Discovery Settings

During the configuration of the scan definition, we did not configure any credentials, which implies that this scan will run unauthenticated.

We also didn't change the default settings of the **ASSESSMENT** menu in the Basic Network Scan template. This means the brute forcing of user credentials will not be done. Even though brute forcing is disabled, our scan generates a significant amount of network traffic and, because we're scanning multiple hosts simultaneously, will be highly noticeable.

Now that we have a basic understanding of how we can customize templates to fit our needs, we can launch our first scan. We can do this by clicking on the arrow next to **Save** and selecting **Launch**.

![Figure 20: Launching the Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/077fbc8dbc903a41f638a7439b81e773-vulnscan_nessuslaunchscanr.png)

Figure 20: Launching the Scan

Initially, the scan will have a status of **Running** in the Nessus dashboard under **My Scans**.

![Figure 21: Running Scan in the Nessus Dashboard](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/8a2bf7d4d5a8da764d31dcc3147678b6-vulnscan_nessusrunningscan5r.png)

Figure 21: Running Scan in the Nessus Dashboard

Figure 21 shows the running scan and provides the options to stop or pause. Keep in mind that this scan was limited to two TCP ports and ran unauthenticated — it gives us a surface-level view. The results may include false positives, and locally exploitable vulnerabilities will not appear. Once the scan is finished, the status will change to **Completed**.

![Figure 22: Completed Scan in the Nessus Dashboard](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/035d20533f5666f009e5a914933e4432-vulnscan_nessusfinishedscan5r.png)

Figure 22: Completed Scan in the Nessus Dashboard

This concludes our first vulnerability scan with Nessus. In the next Learning Unit, we'll examine the results of the scan.

Let's make sure to not disconnect from the VPN connection or shut down the lab because we will be using the same machines to answer questions in the next section.

Some of the labs require you to start the target machine(s) below.

Please note that the IP addresses assigned to your target machines may not match those referenced in the Module text and video.

Vulnerability Scanning - Performing a Vulnerability Scan - VM Group 1

#### Labs

1. Follow the steps above to create your own unauthenticated vulnerability scan on ports 80 and 443 on the machines from VM Group 1.

2. In Nessus, review the **REPORT** menu of the scan configuration, shown in Figure 15. What is the only enabled option in the *Output* section.

Enter the name of the activated checkbox.

Answer

## 7.2.4. Analyzing the results

With our first scan complete, let's walk through the results dashboard and understand what Nessus is showing us.

Info

Due to the continuous updates of Nessus and its plugins, the scan results can differ slightly. We can click on the scan in the **My Scans** list to get to the results dashboard.

Let's click on the "Basic Vulnerability Scan" to see the results.

![Figure 23: Result Dashboard](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/7d697e79e04be72fdeadc51002dd897b-vulnscan_nessusresultdashboard6r.png)

Figure 23: Result Dashboard

Figure 23 shows the top-level results view, where the **Vulnerabilities**, **Remediations**, **Notes** and **History** tabs each provide a different view on the same scan data.

The initial view displays the **Hosts** page, listing all scanned hosts and providing a visual representation of the vulnerability data.

On the right-hand side, we find summary statistics about the vulnerability scan.

The bottom-right panel displays a chart showing the distribution of vulnerability severities across all targets.

Nessus plugins are frequently updated. Therefore, the findings, groupings, and information presented in this Learning Unit may differ from the results of your vulnerability scans.

To view the results of a specific host, we can click on a list entry. This shows us the list of vulnerabilities from the selected host. Let's click on the entry for *192.168.X.13*.

![Figure 24: Vulnerability Result Dashboard of 192.168.50.13](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/03cb9520d717141795403b27a0a8dd92-vulnscan_nessusresultdashboardvuln6r.png)

Figure 24: Vulnerability Result Dashboard of 192.168.50.13

Figure 24 shows the per-host vulnerability list. Notice the two **MIXED** severity entries — these are grouped findings. The **Severity** column gives a quick indicator of each finding's risk level, and the **Count** column shows how many individual vulnerabilities each group contains. We can click on a grouped finding to display the full list.

Let's click on **Apache HTTP Server (Multiple Issues)**, which is listed as **CGI Abuses** under the **Family** column.

![Figure 25: List of Grouped Findings](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/f5bfb7d1008ff8ce02e2d68b50c5e098-vulnscan_nessusgroupedfindings5r.png)

Figure 25: List of Grouped Findings

Figure 25 shows us information on the findings, which were previously grouped. We can get more information by clicking on a finding. Let's click on **Apache 2.4.49 < 2.4.50 Path Traversal Vulnerability**.

![Figure 26: Detailed Information of a Finding](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/dc67dc189272049d8df1be4626ba6964-vulnscan_nessusgroupedfindingssingle4r.png)

Figure 26: Detailed Information of a Finding

Each finding provides detailed information about the vulnerability and the plugin that detected it, including risk ratings, exploit status, and references for further research.

Next, navigate back to the scan results dashboard to review the discovered hosts. The dashboard lists all scanned hosts, as shown in Figure 23.

The next page we'll examine is **Remediations**. If Nessus detects a vulnerability, the plugins often contain a remediation strategy or information on how to mitigate the vulnerability. In the case of the Apache vulnerabilities from Figure 24, we get the following information. It also lists the SSH Terrapin Prefix Truncation Weakness (CVE-2023-48795).

![Figure 27: Remediation of Vulnerabilities](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/1328331263a6ea685db0724e927c358e-vulnscan_nessusresultreme6r.png)

Figure 27: Remediation of Vulnerabilities

The last report page is **History**. This page lists all vulnerability scans with this configuration. We can use it to review or compare the results of previous scans. Since this is our first scan, it only lists one.

Now that the scan is complete, let's review the *Configure* button. Let's click *Configure* at the top to modify its settings. Here we can add additional IP address, change ports and other configuration steps.

We will use this feature later when answering the questions. For example, we can add port `8080`, run the scan to answer one question, then modify the configuration again to scan port `9999`. We can then save it an run the scan again. These additional scans do not count against our final remaining scan.

In the last two sections, we performed a vulnerability scan, and reviewed the results. We can get more familiar with Nessus by customizing the scan configurations and analyzing how the scanning behavior and results differ.

Vulnerability Scanning - Performing a Vulnerability Scan - VM #5

#### Labs

1. In the section "Performing a Vulnerability scan" we launched a vulnerability scan on four target machines. Follow the steps outlined in this section to review the results of this scan and analyze the **Apache 2.4.49 < 2.4.51 Path Traversal Vulnerability** finding. The finding contains a field named **Exploit Code Maturity**. Enter the value of this field as an answer to this exercise.

Answer

2. Now go back to our inital scan, and add port 8080 and perform an unauthenticated vulnerability scan on the same four machines (VM Group 1) as in the previous exercise. Once the scan finishes, review the scan results and identify a host with the grouped finding **HTTP (Multiple Issues)**. Find *HTTP Server Type and Version* and enter the *Jetty* version found.

Answer

3. Nessus can build a *Sitemap* of a target web server. A Sitemap provides information about the content of a web server (e.g. pages). Nessus builds it by crawling through links it identifies on pages. Scan the four target machines ( VM Group 1) on port 9999 with the **Basic Network Scan** template. To configure Nessus to build a Sitemap, click on the **Assessment** settings in the scan configuration and select **Custom** in the dropdown menu. Next, click on *Web Applications* and activate **Scan web applications**. After this, launch the scan. Once it is finished, review the results and analyze the findings on the **Web Application Sitemap** . The output provides a list of found pages. On one of these pages, you will find the flag.

Answer

4. The victim machine VM #5 is running a server that contains several vulnerabilities. One vulnerability allows for directory traversal and arbitrary file access on the target system. Use Nessus to perform a **Basic Network Scan** of the victim. Make sure to configure the scan to assess **all ports**. Once the scan is completed, examine the discovered vulnerability in the results. As a part of the scan, Nessus was able to read the file `C:\Windows\win.ini` as a proof of concept of this exploit. Expand the vulnerability results to view the full content of the retrieved file and locate the embedded flag.

Answer

## 7.2.5. Performing an authenticated vulnerability scan

In this section, we will perform an authenticated vulnerability scan by providing credentials to Nessus. As we discussed previously, authenticated scans produce more detailed information and reduce the number of false positives. To demonstrate this, we will use an authenticated vulnerability scan against the target *DESKTOP*.

Caution

We need to consider that an authenticated scan not only creates a lot of traffic on the network but also a huge amount of noise on the system itself, such as log entries and antivirus (AV) notifications.

To begin, we'll click **New Scan** on the Nessus dashboard.

![Figure 28: Creating a new Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/0c4a555ea4542d0c738969e4c9bd56b7-vulnscan_nessusnewscanr.png)

Figure 28: Creating a new Scan

Even though all Nessus templates accept user credentials, we'll use the **Credentialed Patch Audit** scan template, which comes preconfigured to execute local security checks against the target.

The difference between this and the Basic Network Scan template with provided credentials is that the Credentialed Patch Audit scan only uses local security checks and will not do a regular vulnerability check from an external perspective. The Credentialed Patch Audit template will not only scan for missing operating system patches but also for outdated applications, which may be vulnerable to [*privilege escalation attacks*](https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/cybersecurity-101/cyberattacks/privilege-escalation/).

![Figure 29: Select Credentialed Patch Audit](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/80d3e0faa5b04054ac668c42383137f0-vulnscan_nessustemplateselectauthr.png)

Figure 29: Select Credentialed Patch Audit

Once again, we will provide a name for the scan and set the target to *DESKTOP*.

![Figure 30: Basic Settings for the Authenticated Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/372de43e3eae018a086cf3a8a627fa83-vulnscan_nessusauthtargets5r.png)

Figure 30: Basic Settings for the Authenticated Scan

Next, let's click on the **Credentials** tab and select [**SSH**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secure_Shell) in the **Host** category. On the **Authentication method** dropdown, we'll select **password** and enter "offsec" as the username and "lab" as the password. We'll select **sudo** for the **Elevate privileges with** option and enter "root" as the sudo user and "lab" as the password.

![Figure 31: SSH and Sudo Credentials for the Authenticated Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/b6969bc2eab157d6e131b5c890a35130-vulnscan_nessusauthsshcredsr.png)

Figure 31: SSH and Sudo Credentials for the Authenticated Scan

While we will use the SSH configuration for this example, there are several other authentication mechanisms available. To get a list of all available mechanisms, we can click the **CATEGORIES** dropdown menu and select *All* (currently it is set as *host*). We can consult the [*Tenable Documentation*](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/Credentials.htm) for a complete list of supported authentication mechanisms.

Info

For Linux and macOS targets, SSH is used. While we can also use SSH on Windows, in most cases, we will use [*Server Message Block*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Server_Message_Block) (SMB) and [*Windows Management Instrumentation*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_Management_Instrumentation) (WMI) to perform authenticated vulnerability scans against Windows targets. Both methods allow us to use local or domain accounts and different authentication options.

To get meaningful results in an authenticated vulnerability scan, we need to ensure that our target system is configured correctly. Depending on the authentication method we want to use, we need to make sure that there is no firewall blocking connections from our scanner. Furthermore, we often find AV programs installed on both Linux and Windows targets. AV may flag the vulnerability scan as malicious and therefore terminate our connection or render the results useless. This is an important operational assumption: authenticated scans expect the target environment to be configured to allow the scanner through. Unexpected AV behavior or UAC restrictions are common sources of incomplete results. Depending on the AV program, we can add an [*exception*](https://community.tenable.com/s/article/Symantec-Endpoint-Protection-interfering-with-Nessus-authenticated-scans) for the authenticated scan or temporarily disable it.

Another Windows security technology we need to consider is [*User Account Control*](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/identity-protection/user-account-control/user-account-control-overview) (UAC). UAC is a security feature for Windows that allows users to use standard privileges instead of administrator privileges. An administrative user will run most applications and commands with standard privileges and receive administrator privileges only when needed.

Due to the nature of UAC, it can also interfere with our scan. [We can configure UAC to allow Nessus or temporarily disable it](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/EnableWindowsLoginsForLocalAndRemoteAudits.htm). We should consult the [*Tenable Documentation*](https://docs.tenable.com/nessus/Content/CredentialedChecksOnWindows.htm), especially for Windows targets before we start our first authenticated scan.

Our scan target is a Linux system without AV. Therefore, we can click the arrow next to **Save** and launch the scan.

After the scan has finished, we can review the results.

On the **Vulnerabilities** page, we get a list of the findings for the authenticated scan. In the last section, we had grouped findings with the **MIXED** severity. For our authenticated scan, let's disable the grouping of findings by clicking on the wheel and selecting **Disable Groups**.

![Figure 32: Disable Grouped Results](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/903bc7f5d2513b0b32dd324f2f8d6b97-vulnscan_disablegrouping6r.png)

Figure 32: Disable Grouped Results

After we disable groups, each finding is listed separately.

![Figure 33: Authenticated Scan Results](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/44c33e4fa15fbedcbbf3529dc8d08393-vulnscan_nessusauthresults4r.png)

Figure 33: Authenticated Scan Results

The results include vulnerabilities from the [*Ubuntu Local Security Checks*](https://www.tenable.com/plugins/nessus/families/Ubuntu%20Local%20Security%20Checks) [*plugin family*](https://www.tenable.com/plugins/nessus/families).

Plugins grouped into plugin families check for vulnerabilities in the same context. For example, there are separate plugin families for checking vulnerabilities in databases, firewalls, or web servers. The Ubuntu Local Security Checks plugin family contains a multitude of plugins that check for local vulnerabilities and missing patches for Ubuntu.

The **Name** column provides us with the vulnerable Ubuntu versions and a brief description as well as the patch number for the vulnerabilities.

![Figure 34: Vulnerability data of Firefox and curl](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/723fff9190c423bc4a8f154b77501f9d-vulnscan_nessusauthresultsffcurl2.png)

Figure 34: Vulnerability data of Firefox and curl

The list also contains vulnerability data of locally exposed applications such as [*Firefox*](https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/firefox/new/) or [*cURL*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CURL).

Comparing these results with the earlier unauthenticated scan highlights the difference in depth. The unauthenticated scan revealed only externally reachable service vulnerabilities, while the authenticated scan surfaces locally installed packages, missing OS patches, and outdated applications — none of which are visible from the network alone.

The report identifies missing patches, with one listed as **Critical**, specifically related to the *Heimdal* package. Can you provide the corresponding *Ubuntu Security Notice* (USN) number (answer in USN-####-#)?

## 7.2.6. Working with Nessus plugins

By default, Nessus will enable several plugins behind the scenes when running a default template. While this is certainly useful in many scenarios, we can also fine-tune our options to quickly run a single plugin. We can use this feature to validate a previous finding or to quickly discover all the targets in an environment that are at risk of a specific vulnerability.

For this example, we will set a plugin filter to identify whether the Linux *DESKTOP* machine (VM #1) is vulnerable to [*CVE-2021-3156*](https://www.tenable.com/cve/CVE-2021-3156). This is a locally exploitable vulnerability that allows an unprivileged user to elevate privileges to root.

To leverage the dynamic plugin filter, we will once again begin with a **New Scan**.

![Figure 35: Creating a new Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/0c4a555ea4542d0c738969e4c9bd56b7-vulnscan_nessusnewscanr.png)

Figure 35: Creating a new Scan

This time, we will use the Advanced Dynamic Scan template. This template allows us to use a dynamic plugin filter instead of manually enabling or disabling plugins.

To use this template, we click on **Advanced Dynamic Scan**.

![Figure 36: Select Advanced Dynamic Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/b5bb5bf9f928e72593cc2e6f88ed105a-vulnscan_nessustemplateselectdynr.png)

Figure 36: Select Advanced Dynamic Scan

Once again, we'll configure the name and target.

![Figure 37: Enter Name and Target](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/fd30632c4a5556688b51e8dd925ee611-vulnscan_nessusdynamictarget4r.png)

Figure 37: Enter Name and Target

Next, we'll provide the same SSH and sudo credentials we used in the last example, meaning we'll also be conducting an authenticated scan.

Now we can select the plugins we want to use in our vulnerability scan. As stated before, the Advanced Dynamic Scan allows us to use a filter instead of enabling or disabling groups or individual plugins.

To do so, let's click on the **Dynamic Plugins** tab. In the left dropdown menu, we'll select *CVE* to filter for a specific CVE. In the middle dropdown menu, we can choose from different filter arguments to specify the matching behavior. On the right dropdown menu, we can specify a CVE number. After entering "CVE-2021-3156", we can click on **Preview Plugins**. This may take a few minutes to complete.

![Figure 38: Filter for specific Plugins](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/d64534ef56b209dcf1042f3affc156c3-vulnscan_nessusdynfilterr.png)

Figure 38: Filter for specific Plugins

Once **Preview Plugins** is finished running, we get a list of found plugin families that cover this CVE.

![Figure 39: Select Family of Plugins covering CVE-2021-3156](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/873a30f2f46ce71812a4d4f27abb866f-vulnscan_nessusdynfiltercver.png)

Figure 39: Select Family of Plugins covering CVE-2021-3156

One very useful feature of the dynamic plugin filter is the ability to combine multiple filters. In this example, we know that the target is an Ubuntu Linux system, and we can therefore use a second filter to specify the related plugin family. Let's add a new filter by clicking on the **plus** button next to the first filter.

![Figure 40: Add Filter](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/55589308a3a3bea86e9a633e970011d4-vulnscan_nessusaddfilter2r.png)

Figure 40: Add Filter

A new plugin filter appears. To restrict the plugin family to specific checks for Ubuntu, let's select **Plugin Family** on the left dropdown and **Ubuntu Local Security Checks** on the right dropdown.

![Figure 41: Combined Plugin Filters](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/1312cd53da4adc438df3126597c509cd-vulnscan_nessusttwofilterr.png)

Figure 41: Combined Plugin Filters

Depending on the version of Nessus, the dropdown menu on the right may not display any values. In this case, we can remove the second plugin filter and proceed without it.

We can then click on **Preview Plugins** again to list the plugins determined by our filters. After it completes, let's click on the dropdown and choose **Ubuntu Local Security Checks**. Nessus displays information about the plugin, including affected Ubuntu versions, a short description, and patch number, as well as the Plugin ID.

![Figure 42: Ubuntu Local Security Check Plugin for CVE-2021-3156](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/050f144688a2dae00988f1f0a9e62588-vulnscan_nessusdyncveselect2r.png)

Figure 42: Ubuntu Local Security Check Plugin for CVE-2021-3156

We can get more information by clicking on the plugin. Figure 43 shows the detailed information of the specified plugin.

![Figure 43: Detailed Information of Plugin 145463](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/dec715a62aa078c49ff52ef75ffb82b6-vulnscan_singleplugininfor.png)

Figure 43: Detailed Information of Plugin 145463

After closing this window, we can launch the vulnerability scan as we did before.

Once the scan is finished, let's review the results by clicking on the **Vulnerabilities** tab.

![Figure 44: Listed Findings of the Advanced Dynamic Scan](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/89fe2703e3fc61e1baa86bd7bd46793f-vulnscan_nessusdynresults7r.png)

Figure 44: Listed Findings of the Advanced Dynamic Scan

The output lists one finding with a **HIGH** severity, which was found by the plugin we specified with our dynamic plugin filter. Figure 45 shows the detailed information of the finding, confirming that the target is vulnerable to CVE-2021-3156.

![Figure 45: Detailed Information about the Findings of the specified Plugins](https://static.offsec.com/offsec-courses/PEN-200/imgs/vulnscan/d76caca1737361cd7854861f2e6d3f58-vulnscan_nessusdyncveres4r.png)

Figure 45: Detailed Information about the Findings of the specified Plugins

The plugin output also contains information stating that Nessus only used the reported version number of the affected application and that it did not try to confirm the vulnerability by exploiting it in any way. In an assessment, we should verify these kinds of results to check whether it is indeed an exploitable vulnerability.

Dynamic plugin filters make it fast to scope a scan to a single CVE, but the result still requires manual verification. Nessus flagged the target based on the reported version number alone — not by attempting to exploit the vulnerability.
